coredata.dc:title: Preparation of carbon dots-doped terbium phosphonate coordination polymers as ratiometric fluorescent probe for citrate detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Bohao
Wang, Yajie
Sun, Mengyao
Luo, Yongquan
Yu, Haoran
Zhang, Lei
coredata.dc:description: In this work, carbon dots-doped terbium phosphonate coordination polymers (CDs-GMP/Tb) were designed and prepared as ratiometric fluorescent probes for the detection of citrate. The as-prepared CDs-GMP/Tb are prepared and have the merits of high photostability, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility. The as-prepared CDs-GMP/Tb as ratiometric fluorescent probes also have better anti-interference ability and stability compared with the traditional single fluorescent probe. The surface morphology, fabrication, and spectroscopy were characterized through a variety of instruments. It confirms that the probes exhibited network structure doping carbon dots. With the addition of citrate, the fluorescence of GMP/Tb at 545 nm was significantly quenched, contrasting to the enhancement of fluorescence of CDs at 454 nm. Under optimum conditions, the detection limit for citrate was 0.47 μM, with a linear range of 0–200 μM between citrate concentrations and I545/I454. It has high sensitivity, selective, and rapid detection for citrate. The as-prepared CDs-GMP/Tb as ratiometric fluorescent probes were also used for imaging citrate in living cells. These experiment results showed that CDs-GMP/Tb as ratiometric fluorescent probes could be applied to trace citrate detection in the environmental and biological fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Terbium coordination polymers
Ratiometric
Fluorescent probe
Citrate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon dots-doped coordination polymers (CDs-GMP/Tb)
Fluorescence spectra analysis of Cit as fluorescence probe of CDs-GMP/Tb
Interference of CDs-GMP/Tb for detection of Cit
The fluorescence spectroscopy of CDs-GMP/Tb to Cit

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM of CDs (a), GMP/Tb (b) and CDs-GMP/Tb (c); SEM of GMP/Tb (d) and CDs-GMP/Tb (e).
FTIR spectra of CDs (a), GMP/Tb (b) and CDs-GMP/Tb (c).
Absorption spectra of CDs, GMP/Tb, CDs-GMP/Tb and CDs-GMP/Tb with Cit and Emission spectra of CDs, CDs-GMP/Tb and CDs-GMP/Tb with Cit.
XPS spectra of CDs-GMP/Tb (a), C element (b), N element (c), Tb element (d), and O element (e).
Fluorescent emission decay curves of CDs-GMP/Tb and CDs -GMP/Tb with Cit (Emission at 545 nm) (a); Emission spectra of CDs, and CDs with Cit (b).
Effects of various chemicals (200 μM) on fluorescence intensity of CDs-GMP/Tb without (red) and with 200 μM Cit (black) in HEPES buffer (25 mM, pH 7.0).

The emission spectra of CDs-GMP/Tb solution with Cit of different concentration: 0 μM, 5 μM, 10 μM, 20 μM, 40 μM, 60 μM, 80 μM, 100 μM, 140 μM, 160 μM, 200 μM, 300 μM (Monitored and excited at 545/454 nm and 290 nm, respectively).
Fluorescence responses of CDs-GMP/Tb solution to various Cit concentrations (a), Linear relationship of fluorescence intensity at 544 and 454 nm as the response of Cit concentration (b).

Confocal fluorescence imaging of HepG-2 cells. HepG-2 cells incubated with CDs-GMP/Tb for 30 min (e-h); and pre-incubated with 50 μM Cit for 30 min followed by incubation with CDs-GMP/Tb for another 30 min (a-d). (a) merged of b-d; (e) merged of f-h; (b, f) blue channel; (c, g) green channel; (d, h) bright field; blue channel excited with a 404 nm laser; green channel excited with a 488 nm laser.
Schematic description of Cit sensing by using CDs-GMP/Tb as fluorescent probe.

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon dots-doped terbium phosphonate coordination polymers as ratiometric fluorescent probe for citrate detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry, School of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry, School of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, Anhui Medical College, Hefei 230601, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacy, Anhui Medical College, Hefei 230601, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots-doped terbium phosphonate coordination polymers (CDs-GMP/Tb) were designed and prepared as ratiometric fluorescent probes for the detection of citrate.

In this work, carbon dots-doped terbium phosphonate coordination polymers (CDs-GMP/Tb) were designed and prepared as ratiometric fluorescent probes for the detection of citrate. The as-prepared CDs-GMP/Tb are prepared and have the merits of high photostability, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility. The as-prepared CDs-GMP/Tb as ratiometric fluorescent probes also have better anti-interference ability and stability compared with the traditional single fluorescent probe. The surface morphology, fabrication, and spectroscopy were characterized through a variety of instruments. It confirms that the probes exhibited network structure doping carbon dots. With the addition of citrate, the fluorescence of GMP/Tb at 545 nm was significantly quenched, contrasting to the enhancement of fluorescence of CDs at 454 nm. Under optimum conditions, the detection limit for citrate was 0.47 μM, with a linear range of 0–200 μM between citrate concentrations and I545/I454. It has high sensitivity, selective, and rapid detection for citrate. The as-prepared CDs-GMP/Tb as ratiometric fluorescent probes were also used for imaging citrate in living cells. These experiment results showed that CDs-GMP/Tb as ratiometric fluorescent probes could be applied to trace citrate detection in the environmental and biological fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Citrate is one of the widely used tricarboxylic acids in the food and pharmaceutical industries as well known. For example, it is used as anti-bacteria in the food industries [1] and anticoagulant in infusion [2]. Citrate also plays a key role as an essential intermediate during the Krebs cycle in the metabolism of aerobic organisms [3,4]. Citrate has also been a key biomarker for monitoring several diseases such as urolithiasis and prostate cancer [5-7]. For example, citrate levels reduced in urine are lower than in healthy people, it is correlated to some features of kidney dysfunction such as prostate cancer, nephrolithiasis [7,8]. Therefore, it is significant to develop a selective and sensitive analytical method for detection of citrate in the complex chemical and biological samples aspects of clinical diagnosis and chemical analysis.

To date, lots of conventional methods about citrate detection are developed such as ion-exchange chromatography [9,10], high performance liquid chromatography [11] electrochemical methods [12,13] and spectrometry [14,15]. But, these methods usually have the drawbacks such as requirements of complicated sample pretreatment, time-consuming and valuable instruments. Recently, fluorescent probes as chemical sensors were quickly developed to monitor citrate in environmental and biological samples, due to the merits of high sensitivity, easy operation, fast response, and inexpensive instrumentation [16]. So far, several fluorescent methods have been published for the detection of citrate listed as Quantum Dots [17], traditional organic dyes [18] and lanthanide-based coordination polymers [19]. These traditional single-signal fluorescent probes such as organic dyes generally have lower interference tolerance in the complex environment and are easily bleached by light. At the same time, the fluorescent probes of heavy metal quantum dots and their coordination polymers have high biological toxicity and need complex synthesis.

Lanthanide-based coordination polymers were composed of lanthanide ions and correlative ligands. Guanosine 5′-monophosphate disodium salt hydrate (GMP) is one kind of the widely used ligands to the terbium ions. It has good performance on the interaction between terbium ions and phosphate group and the N7 of pyrimidine of GMP [20,21]. The fluorescent probes of LnCPs as chemical sensors have the merits of long lifetimes, sharply spiked emission bands and large Stokes shifts [22,23]. Carbon Dots have proper properties such as tunable emission and excitation wavelength, high solubility in water, high stability, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [24,25], which led to a wide application in numerous photo-catalyst [26,27], optical sensors [28] and biosensors and biomedical imaging [29,30] fields.

To solve the drawback of conventional traditional single-signal fluorescent probes, lots of ratiometric fluorescent probes are developed such as FRET ratiometric fluorescent probe (CMH-GA-CDs) [31], FH-GA-CQDs [32], and so on [33]. Herein, terbium-modified fluorescent CDs are prepared with the merits of high photostability, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility. For instance, there are several reported methods for preparing CDs-Tb(III) complex in application of sensing [34,35]. Chen at el [34] prepared fluorescent probes through carboxyl and amine groups on the surface of CDs chelating to Tb(III) ions. However, in our work, carbon Dots-doped terbium phosphonate coordination polymers (CDs-GMP/Tb) are prepared with different methods. Firstly, the terbium phosphonate coordination polymers are prepared and exhibit network structure with GMP and Tb(III). Thus, the ratiometric fluorescent probe is prepared through incorporating carbon dots into the polymers. Contrary to the dots structure of CDs-Tb(III) complex, the prepared CDs-GMP/Tb exhibited network structure using GMP as ligand. The CDs-GMP/Tb as a ratiometric fluorescent probe has the following unique characteristics. The as-prepared CDs-GMP/Tb were exhibited network structure doping carbon dots with the two different emission peaks assigned to GMP/Tb and Carbon dots. They also had the advantage of sharply emission bands, long fluorescent lifetimes and large stoke shifts which benefited from LnCPs. In addition, they can make the luminescent intensity of CDs enhance, while weakening the luminescent intensity of GMP/Tb with the addition of Cit as a ratiometric fluorescent probe. As shown in the process of Scheme 1 , the as-prepared CDs-GMP/Tb as ratiometric fluorescent probes have high sensitivity, selective and rapid detection for citrate.

Terbium nitrate (Tb(NO3)3, 99.99%) and Guanosine 5′-(disodium phosphate) (GMP) were obtained from J&K (Beijing, China). Anhydrous sodium citrate, N-2-hydroxyethylpiperazine-N-2-ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES), ethanol, and urea were shopped for Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The solution of cation and anion (KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, NaSO4, NaCl, CH3COONa), glucose, oxalic acid, isocitric acid, succinic acid and ascorbic acid were analytical reagent grade and also purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagents Company (Shanghai, China).

The carbon dots-doped coordination polymers (CDs-GMP/Tb) structure and surface morphology were performed by ultrahigh-resolution field emission scanning electron microscopy (HR-SEM, S-3400N, JEOL, Japan). CDs particle sizes and network structure of CDs-GMP/Tb were characterized with transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). The Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) of CDs, GMP/Tb and CDs-GMP/Tb were characterized with Thermo Nicolet 380 spectrometer through KBr pellet method (Nicolet, Wisconsin, USA). The fluorescence lifetime of CDs-GMP/Tb had been performed on Fluorescence Spectrofluorometer (FLS980, Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The absorption spectra were scanned by Evolution 220 UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific). The confocal imaging experiment was completed on laser confocal fluorescence imaging (A1R, Nikon, Japan).

Carbon dots were prepared according to a hydrothermal method as followed. Briefly, 2.746 g anhydrous sodium citrate and 3.362 g urea was dissolved in pure water (20 mL) to gain a clear transparent solution. Then, the solution was transferred to a PTFE-lined autoclave and sealed to react at 185 °C for 6 h. After that, the resulting solution became a yellow color solution when it was cooled down at room temperature. Next, the following yellow reaction solution was transferred to the dialysis bag (500 MWC) for 24 h. Last, the carbon dots were gained through the freeze drying and kept until further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots-doped coordination polymers (CDs-GMP/Tb)
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots-doped coordination polymers (CDs-GMP/Tb) were prepared according to the following procedure. CDs-GMP/Tb was prepared by mixing 3.0 mL of 25 mM GMP, 3.0 mL of 2 mg mL−1 CDs, 1.5 mL of 50 mM Tb(NO3)3 and 7.5 mL of 25 mM HEPES buffer. Reaction solutions were stirred 30 min at room temperature and centrifuged (1000 rpm) for 10 min. The residual was washed by pure water and the precipitation of CDs-GMP/Tb dispersed in 15 mL pure water for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectra analysis of Cit as fluorescence probe of CDs-GMP/Tb
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical process, the stock solution of Cit was prepared through 27.4 mg anhydrous sodium citrate dissolved in the 10 mL H2O. Then, a series of different concentrations of Cit solutions were prepared by dilution. Then, standard solutions of Cit (0–600 μM) were mixed with probes of CDs-GMP/Tb (200 μL). After that, each mixed suspension solution was diluted to 2 mL with 25 mM HEPES buffer (pH 7.0) and shocked for 15 s. Finally, the standard solution with Cit was scanned by fluorescence spectra with excitement at 290 nm through a quartz optical cell after 5.5 min later. The maximum emission wavelengths of CDs and GMP/Tb were at 454 and 545 nm, respectively. The relative standard deviations of fluorescence spectra were scanned by three measurements for one sample.

Imaging in living cells experiment was through HepG-2 cell lines cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (High glucose). The DMEM was in an incubator at 37 °C which contained 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin in an atmosphere of 5 % CO2. The cells were transferred to confocal dishes and incubated in the incubator for 24 h to let the cells adhere to the wall. The cells were cultured with a serum-free DEME medium containing probe after cleaning with PBS. Then, cells were incubated for 30 min and cleaned with PBS again to move the residual probe. For control experiments, the cells were incubated with 50 μM Cit and washed with PBS (20 mM, pH 7.4). Then, cells were incubated with CDs-GMP/Tb for 30 min. The fluorescence signals of the green and blue channel were collected with 488 nm and 404 nm (Ex), respectively.

The carbon dots-doped coordination polymers (CDs-GMP/Tb) structure and surface morphology were imaged with TEM (Fig. 1 ). As shown in Fig. 1a, particle size distribution of carbon dots is about 2.0–7.0 nm. The average particle size is around 4.8 nm. The terbium phosphonate coordination polymers (GMP/Tb) were a network structure (Fig. 1b). Because of the too small particle size of carbon dots, the carbon dots-doped terbium phosphonate coordination polymers (CDs-GMP/Tb) have no obvious change in the TEM image (Fig. 1c). The surface morphology of CDs-GMP/Tb was studied by SEM as shown in Fig. 1e. When carbon dots doped in coordination polymers, the morphology of CDs-GMP/Tb was obviously kept the same structure with the GMP/Tb (Fig. 1 d) as shown in Fig. 1 d, e. The preparation of CDs-GMP/Tb is originated from the self-assembly of Tb(III) and GMP in HEPES buffer to form the network structure of CDs-GMP. So, the CDs-GMP/Tb were still kept in the network structure with the doping carbon dots.

FTIR spectra were used to measure the chemical compositions of CDs, GMP/Tb and CDs-GMP/Tb as shown in Fig. 2 . There were obvious characteristic symmetric stretching bands at 1587 cm−1 and antisymmetric stretching bands at 1400 cm−1 assigned to the COO of the surface of CDs (Fig. 2 a). The OH group at 3413 cm−1 was ascribed to the H2O of the surface of CDs. Two typical symmetric and antisymmetric stretching bands at 998 and 1112 cm−1 were assigned to phosphate of GMP/Tb (Fig. 2 b). When the CDs doped GMP/Tb, three characteristic bands at 1631, 1111 and 996 cm−1 were obviously observed in the Fig. 2 c. Because the GMP/Tb and CDs had the similar groups of CO, the CDs-GMP/Tb had the similar IR spectra of GMP/Tb when CDs successfully doped the structure of GMP/Tb.

The absorption and emission spectra were also performed to research the composition of CDs-GMP/Tb as shown Fig. 3 a, b. The strong absorption peak was at 336 nm of the COO because of CDs. The strong absorption peak at 253 nm was assigned to GMP [36], which appeared in the coordination polymers of GMP/Tb and CDs-GMP/Tb. There was a strong absorption peak at 253 nm contrast to a weak absorption peak at 336 nm with the CDs successfully doped the structure of GMP/Tb. The absorption peak of CDs-GMP/Tb at 212 nm was shifted to 203 nm with the addition of Cit. It indicated the interaction between Tb(III) and Cit. As shown in Fig. 3 b, the emission peak at 454 nm assigned CDs was increased and decreased dramatically at 545 nm assigned GMP/Tb with the addition of 200 μM Cit. Therefore, CDs-GMP/Tb is suitable as the ratiometric fluorescent probe for detection Cit.

The XPS spectroscopy was used to research chemical states of the fluorescent probe. The CDs-GMP/Tb has appeared characteristic peaks of Tb3d (1241.81 eV), O1s (531.52 eV), N1s (399.13 eV) and C1s (285.01 eV) as shown Fig. 4 . The main peaks of C-O-C bonding from carbon dots and GMP were observed in the 285.01 eV (C1s) and 531.52 eV (O1s). The main peaks at 399.13 eV (N1s) were assigned to the CNC bonding, which were from the structure of GMP. Two spin-orbits as 3d5/2 (1242.48 eV) and 3d3/2 (1276.78 eV) with a binding energy difference of 34.3 eV were ascribed to the split of Tb3d XPS spectrum. It indicated that Tb(III) was combined with GMP.

The fluorescence lifetime of CDs-GMP/Tb was researched with the addition of Cit through Fluorescence Spectro- fluorometer to investigate interaction of Cit with CDs-GMP/Tb. The lifetimes of CDs-GMP/Tb and CDs-GMP/Tb with addition of Cit were 576.96 μs and 630.80 μs as shown in Fig. 5 a, respectively. From the fluorescence spectroscopy of CDs and CDs with Cit (Fig. 5 b), Cit has no effect on the fluorescence spectrum of CDs. It indicated that Cit could not be interaction with CDs. Therefore, Cit might be as co-ligand with GMP to combine with Tb(III) ions to form the complex. The results indicated that Cit might be coordinated with Tb(III) and enhance the fluorescence lifetime of CDs-GMP/Tb.

For development of the best detection method based on CDs-GMP/Tb, the experimental parameters including excitation and emission, pH and reaction time were investigated to develop an analytical method for Cit. The optimal parameters were as listed: (a) excitation and emission wavelength; (b) pH and (c) Interaction time. The optimal experimental conditions were studied in the Supporting Materials. The optimal condition of excitation and emission wavelength was fixed at 290 nm (Ex), 454 nm (Em) (assigned CDs) and 545 nm (Em) (assigned Tb(III)). The optimal condition of the pH was 7.0 in 25 mM HEPES buffer. 5.5 min was the optimal interaction time for detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference of CDs-GMP/Tb for detection of Cit
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the anti-interference ability of the procedure developed, the interference of various chemicals including cation and anion (K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl-, SO4 2-, CH3COO−), urea, glucose, ascorbic acid, oxalic acid, isocitric acid and succinic acid were examined to test the change of fluorescence intensity about CDs-GMP/Tb as the optimal experimental condition. It had almost a little influence for detection of Cit as interference of cation, anion, glucose and urea as shown in Fig. 6 . However, the ratio at I545/I454 of fluorescence intensity was slightly decreased with the addition of ascorbic acid, the ratio at I545/I454 of fluorescence intensity reached the same level as blank after addition of Cit. Therefore, the results illustrated that CDs-GMP/Tb has a good anti-interference ability for detection of Cit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence spectroscopy of CDs-GMP/Tb to Cit
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-GMP/Tb as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for detection of Cit was examined under the optimal condition through fluorescence spectroscopy. The ratio at I545/I454 of fluorescence intensity of CDs-GMP/Tb (200 μL, pH 7.0) was gradually reduced with the addition of concentrations of Cit solution as shown Fig. 7 . Under the 290 nm excitation wavenumber, the CDs-GMP/Tb were luminescent with a strong emission peak at 545 nm contrast to a weak peak at 454 nm. The emission peak at 545 nm assigned to Tb ions was originated electron energy transition from 5D4 to 7F5. With the addition of Cit into the CDs-GMP/Tb, Cit might be as co-ligand with GMP to combine with Tb(III) ions. The result is the enhanced luminescent intensity of CDs and the reduced luminescent intensity of GMP/Tb (Fig. 8 a). The ratio at I545/I454 of fluorescence intensity was reached equilibrium, when the concentration of Cit added to 300 μM. Therefore, Cit concentration and the ratio at I545/I454 of fluorescence intensity had a good linear relationship during concentration from 0 to 200 μM (Fig. 8 b). The linear curve was I545/I454 = 5.30831–0.02459C and correlation coefficient (R2) were reached to 0.9986 during the range of 0, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 140, 160 and 200 μM. The developed method for detection of Cit had detection limit of 0.47 μM using CDs-GMP/Tb as a ratiometric fluorescence probe. The RSD was 3.98 % (c = 40 μM, n = 5). Among the LnCPs fluorescent probes, this developed detection method using CDs-GMP/Tb as the ratiometric fluorescence probe had better detection sensitivity compared to published methods shown in Table S1.

The application of CDs-GMP/Tb as fluorescent probes is imaging to detect Cit in living cells. As shown in Fig. 9 , the evaluation was through the contrast images between HepG-2 cells with Cit and without Cit using CDs-GMP/Tb as a ratiometric fluorescent probe. The results indicated that images (Fig. 9 g, f) emitted obviously green light and twilight blue light after HepG-2 cells incubated for 30 min using CDs-GMP/Tb as fluorescent probe. Thus, the contrast results indicated that images (Fig. 9 b, c) appeared the obviously blue fluorescence enhancing and the quenching green light with the 30 min incubation by 50 μM Cit. This proved that fluorescent image was one of applications for CDs-GMP/Tb as a ratiometric fluorescent probe.

In our work, a hydrothermal method was used to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) by urea and trisodium citrate (anhydrous). Then, CDs were doped into the terbium phosphonate coordination polymers to form ratiometric fluorescent probes (CDs-GMP/Tb) for citrate detection. The as-prepared CDs-GMP/Tb was studied through morphology structure and chemical composition analysis, and exhibited network structure doping carbon dots. From absorption and fluorescence spectra, the fluorescence of CDs at 454 nm showed obvious enhancement with addition of citrate, while the fluorescence of GMP/Tb at 545 nm was significantly quenched. The analytical method was evaluated by quantitative analysis of citrate under optimal conditions. The Cit concentration and the ratio at I545/I454 of fluorescence intensity had a good linear relationship during concentration from 0 to 200 μM under the optimal condition. The developed method for detection of Cit had detection limit of 0.47 μM, the correlation coefficient of 0.9986. What’s more, fluorescent image detection was successfully realized using CDs-GMP/Tb as ratiometric fluorescent probes. These experiment results showed that CDs-GMP/Tb could be applied to trace citrate detection in environmental and biological fields.

Bohao Yu: Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yajie Wang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Mengyao Sun: Investigation. Yongquan Luo: Investigation, Formal analysis. Haoran Yu: Investigation. Lei Zhang: Conceptualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21707034), Natural Science Foundation of Educational Department of Anhui Province (No. KJ2020A0856), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 222201814020) and Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (No. 20142200900).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120656.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
